root.question: What are you seeing in or around the hive?

options.label: Very few bees — colony seems much smaller
Many dead bees in front of the hive
No eggs or young larvae visible inside
Brood looks wrong — discoloured or sunken
Bees clustering on outside of hive (bearding)
Bees are unusually aggressive

few-bees.question: When did you last inspect this hive?

options.label: Less than 2 weeks ago — it was fine then
More than a month ago

few-bees-recent.question: Can you see the queen or fresh eggs (tiny white grains standing upright in cells)?

options.label: Yes, eggs are visible
No eggs, no sign of the queen

actions: Open the hive on a warm calm day (above 12°C).
Check for the queen, eggs, and brood.
Look for varroa mites on adult bees and in cells.
If colony is very weak, consider merging with a stronger hive.

dead-bees.question: What do the dead bees look like?

options.label: Normal looking — just dead, in a pile
Deformed wings, stunted bodies
Bees with stingers out, fighting posture

dead-bees-normal.question: What time of year is it and how many dead bees?

options.label: Winter or early spring — a few hundred
Summer — hundreds or thousands

actions: Some dead bees in winter is normal — the cluster shrinks as bees age.
Clear the entrance so living bees can take cleansing flights on warm days.
Heft the hive to check food stores are still adequate.
Do not open the hive unless temperature is above 12°C.
Note the date and number of dead bees — document this carefully.
Check if nearby fields or gardens were recently sprayed.
Contact your local beekeeping association to report the incident.
Assess the surviving colony — check for eggs and brood to judge colony strength.

no-brood.question: How long since you last saw eggs or brood?

options.label: Just noticed today — haven't checked recently
More than 3 weeks with no brood visible

no-brood-check.diagnosis: Possible queen issue — thorough inspection needed
actions: Inspect all frames methodically in good light.
Look for eggs (tiny grains standing upright in cells — you may need reading glasses).
Eggs are 1–3 days old. If you see them, the queen was active recently.
If no eggs found after a thorough search, assume the queen is lost.
Check for emergency queen cells — bees may already be raising a new queen.
If no queen cells found, you need to introduce a new queen or a frame of fresh eggs from another hive.
Do not wait more than 3–4 weeks or laying workers may develop, making recovery very difficult.
Contact your local beekeeping association for a queen source.

sick-brood.question: What does the sick brood look like?

options.label: Sunken, perforated cappings — bees seem to be chewing them open
Dead larvae, greasy or yellow/brown in colour
Chalk-like white or grey mummies in or around cells

actions: Do NOT transfer any equipment to other hives — AFB spreads easily.
Perform the 'ropiness test': insert a twig into a dead cell and pull slowly — AFB stretches 2–3 cm like caramel.
If positive for AFB, this is a notifiable disease in most countries.
Contact your local beekeeping inspector immediately — do not delay.
EFB often clears up when the nectar flow improves and the colony strengthens.
Ensure the queen is young and productive — requeen if the queen is old.
In severe cases, antibiotic treatment may be available — check local regulations.
Report to your local beekeeping inspector.
Improve hive ventilation — chalkbrood thrives in cold, damp conditions.
Ensure the colony is large enough to cover all brood and keep it warm.
Replace old dark comb — fungal spores persist in wax.
Strong colonies with a young queen usually clear chalkbrood on their own.

bearding.question: What is the weather like right now?

options.label: Hot and humid — above 30°C
Mild weather, not particularly hot

actions: This is normal behaviour in hot weather — bees hang outside to cool the hive.
Ensure there is a nearby water source within 200 metres.
Provide extra ventilation by propping the roof slightly or adding a mesh floor.
The bees will return inside when temperatures drop in the evening.
Inspect the hive for queen cells immediately.
If queen cells are present, the colony is preparing to swarm.
Perform an artificial swarm or remove/destroy queen cells to prevent swarming.
Add more space (a super or brood box) if the hive is congested.

options.label: Yes — was calm before, suddenly very aggressive
Always been a bit defensive

aggressive-sudden.diagnosis: Sudden aggression — possible queenlessness or robbing
actions: Check the hive for the queen and fresh brood — queenless colonies often become aggressive.
Look for signs of robbing: bees fighting at the entrance, suspicious activity from other hives.
If being robbed, reduce the entrance to a single bee width immediately.
Avoid opening the hive during a robbing frenzy — it will make things worse.
Some colonies are naturally more defensive — genetics play a big role.
Consider requeening with a locally-bred, calm queen next spring.
Always use full protection (suit, gloves) and work bees on warm, sunny, windless days.
Use generous smoke before and during inspections.
Do a sugar roll or alcohol wash: count mites from a sample of ~300 bees.
More than 3 mites per 100 bees requires treatment.
Choose a treatment suitable for the season (Apivar strips in active season, oxalic acid in broodless period).
Recount mites 4–6 weeks after treatment to confirm it worked.

varroa-confirmed.diagnosis: Varroa mite infestation — deformed wing virus symptoms
actions: Deformed wings and stunted bees are caused by Deformed Wing Virus spread by varroa mites.
Treat immediately — colony collapse is possible without urgent treatment.
Use Apivar strips (amitraz) in an active colony, or oxalic acid if the colony is broodless.
After treatment, assess whether the colony is strong enough to recover — combine with another if very weak.
Reduce the entrance to a single bee width right now.
Stop all open feeding near the hive immediately.
Do not inspect the hive during a robbing event — it makes it significantly worse.
Once robbing stops, assess the colony's remaining stores and strength.
